does: This paper proposes an Uncertainty-aware Reward Model (URM) that estimates both reward scores and confidence levels for language model responses using Bayesian approximation. Trained on preference datasets, the URM provides dual outputs: a reward signal indicating response quality and an uncertainty measure reflecting confidence in that judgment. The method integrates these uncertainty estimates into language model training through two mechanisms: filtering training data based on confidence to improve curation quality, and modifying policy optimization objectives to account for uncertain assessments. Experiments on Vicuna and MT-bench benchmarks demonstrate substantial improvements in instruction-following capabilities compared to existing approaches.
connects: This work builds on the recent surge in reward model-based training for language models following RLHF and similar preference-learning paradigms. It responds to a recognized limitation in existing reward models: they produce point estimates without capturing confidence, leading to noisy training signals from borderline cases or ambiguous instructions. The paper combines ideas from Bayesian deep learning and uncertainty quantification with reward modeling, drawing on classical methods for calibrating confidence in neural networks. It extends beyond prior work that treats all preference data equally by introducing principled uncertainty weighting, addressing the known problem that inconsistent or low-confidence labels degrade alignment training.
enables: This approach opens directions for more robust reward models that account for epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty in instruction evaluation. It suggests reward-based training systems could benefit from explicit confidence calibration, potentially applying similar uncertainty estimation to other stages of language model development. The work enables better data curation strategies that automatically downweight ambiguous examples, pointing toward curriculum learning approaches where training difficulty adapts based on uncertainty. Future extensions could combine this with active learning to identify which instruction-response pairs most benefit from human re-annotation, or apply uncertainty estimates to detect when models reach the limits of instruction-following training on a given dataset.
